name: Clive Churchill, AM
bio:
**Full Name:** Clive Bernard Churchill, AM.

**Nickname:** The Little Master.

**Born:** 21/1/1927 at Mereweather (Newcastle), NSW.

**Died:** 9/8/1985 at Camperdown (Sydney), NSW [aged 58].

**Juniors:** Marist Brothers Hamilton.

**Height:** 176 cm.

**Weight:** 67-76 kgs.

**PLAYING CAREER WITH THE RABBITOHS**

**Rabbitohs First Grade Player No:** 365. **Rabbitohs First Grade Captain No:** 50.

First Grade premiership 1947-58 (157 games, 13 tries, 72 goals, 5 field goals, 193 pts) – 30 games as captain.

First Grade Country Week 1952-58 (7 games, 8 goals, 16 points) – 1 game as captain

First Grade Domestic Tours 1947-48, 1950-51, 1955, 1958 (1 game v South Australia/12 tour games, 5 tries, 13 goals, 41 points) – 6 games as captain

[Never played Lower Grades]

**FIRST GRADE PREMIERSHIP DEBUT WITH THE RABBITOHS**

Round 17 v Newtown at Erskineville Oval, Erskineville/Gadigal, August 16, 1947 – Age: 20 years and 207 days.

**REPRESENTATIVE HONOURS WITH THE RABBITOHS**

**Kangaroos Player No:** 249. **NSW Blues Player No:** 415.

Australia 1948-56 (37 Tests & World Cup games/49 tour games, 7 tries, 49 goals, 1 field goal, 105 points) – Captain in 27 Tests & World Cup games, 32 tour games.

NSW 1948-55, 1957 (27 games v Queensland/5 games v Great Britain/3 games v France/1 game v New Zealand/2 games v American All Stars/1 tour game, 5 tries, 24 goals, 3 field goals, 69 points) – Captain in 17 games v Queensland, 4 games v Great Britain, 3 games v France, 1 game v NZ, 2 games v American All Stars.

Kangaroos v The Rest 1953 (1 game, 0 pts) – as captain

Australasia v Rugby League XIII 1954 (1 game, 0 pts) – as captain

An Australian XIII v France 1951 (1 game, 1 goal, 2 points) – as captain

Sydney v American All Stars 1952 (1 game, 0 pts) – as captain

City 1948, 1956 (2 games, 0 pts) – 1 game as captain

City Firsts 1949-55 (7 games, 2 tries, 3 goals, 1 field goal, 14 points) – 3 games as captain

City Seconds 1948 (1 game, 0 pts)

Possibles 1948 (1 game, 0 pts)

**COACHING CAREER**

**Rabbitohs First Grade Coach No.** 11.

Australia 1952-53 tour, 1959, 1963

NSW 1952-53

Sydney 1953

Kangaroos 1953

Rabbitohs 1958 (captain-coach)

Norths Brisbane – BRL premiership 1959 (premiers, captain-coach)

Brisbane 1959

Queensland 1959

Moree 1960-61

City Firsts 1960

City Seconds 1960

Canterbury-Bankstown 1963-64

Easts Brisbane 1965-66

Rabbitohs 1967-75 (4 premierships, 1 runner-up)

Gold Coast 1978

**AWARDS & ACCOLADES**

1948-49 Kangaroo Tour

1949 E.E. Christensen Rugby League Yearbook Player of the Year

1950 Claude Corbett Trophy (awarded to winning captain of Third Test v Great Britain)

1950 Rabbitohs First Grade premiership winner

1950 E.E. Christensen Rugby League Yearbook Player of the Year

1951 Rabbitohs First Grade premiership winner

1952 Sunday Herald Best & Fairest

1952 E.E. Christensen Rugby League Yearbook Player of the Year

1952 Sunday Telegraph Player/Star of the Year

1952-53 Kangaroo Tour

1953 Rabbitohs First Grade premiership winner

1953 Sunday Telegraph Player/Star of the Year

1954 Claude Corbett Trophy (awarded to winning captain of Third Test v Great Britain)

1954 Rabbitohs First Grade premiership winner

1954 Grand Final Man of the match (Clive Churchill medal)

1955 Sunday Telegraph Player/Star of the Year

1956-57 Kangaroo Tour

1957 Sunday Telegraph Player/Star of the Year

1959 Norths Brisbane – BRL First Grade premiership winner (captain-coach)

1961 Rabbitohs Life Membership (Inductee No. 4)

1967 Rabbitohs First Grade premiership winner (winning coach)

1968 Rabbitohs First Grade premiership winner (winning coach)

1970 Rabbitohs First Grade premiership winner (winning coach)

1971 Rabbitohs First Grade premiership winner (winning coach)

1981 Immortal (amongst the first four to be picked)

1985 Member of the Order of Australia (AM)

1985 Sport Australia Hall of Fame

1986 Clive Churchill Stand at Sydney Cricket Ground (named after him)

1986 Clive Churchill Medal (named after him)

1992 RLW Top 100 Players of All-time (Ranked No. 1)

1992 Rabbitohs Dream Team (as a Fullback)

2002 NRL Hall of Fame (Inductee No. 43)

2002 International Rugby League Hall of Fame

2008 Australian Team of the Century (as a Fullback)

2008 NSW Team of the Century (as a Fullback)

2008 NSW Country Team of the Century (as a Fullback)

2017 NSWRL Hall of Fame

**ADMINISTRATION**

Rabbitohs Club Selector 1954

NSWRL Metropolitan Selection Committee 1962

NSWRL State Selection Committee 1962

Rabbitohs General Committee 1950-54

Rabbitohs Grading Committee 1958

**BIOGRAPHY**

When Churchill retired as a player in 1959 he played in more Test matches than anybody else, with 34 Tests and 3 World Cup matches, and also more state matches, with 38. His attacking flair as a player is credited with changing the role of the fullback. He was just as effective in defence, consistently chopping down opponents more than twice his size and acting as a great cover defender. He had great hands and was a daredevil on the field; he used to take a lot of risks. This was particularly impressive considering he stood at just 176 centimetres tall and weighed less than 80 kilograms throughout his playing career.

When he was 12, Churchill travelled to Sydney to play in a schoolboy's match with a Newcastle representative team at Sydney Cricket Ground. The late 'Jersey' Flegg, who at the time was president of the New South Wales Rugby League, was at the Sydney Cricket Ground to watch the game and afterwards he spoke with the young schoolboys. Pointing to Churchill, Flegg said: "And over there I see a youngster who wore the number six jersey, and I predict one day he will become an international and captain Australia in Test football." Not only did this bold prediction come true when Churchill played for Australia less than a decade later, and then go on to captain Australia, but he also turned out to be the greatest player of all time.

''He set the standard by which all the famous rugby league players of his era, and since, will be assessed,'' wrote renowned league writer Tom Goodman. Churchill got his nickname from Ray Stehr, who commented: ''I never saw Dally Messenger ('The Master') play,'' said Stehr. ''But I have seen the Little Master.''

On 2 July 1981 the Rugby League Week magazine issued their Immortals, with the four originals – Clive Churchill, Reg Gasnier, John Raper and Bob Fulton – with photos of all four-standing proud in their Australian jerseys on the hallowed turf of the Sydney Cricket Ground. In 1992 Rugby League Week issued their Top 100 all-time players and Clive Churchill was ranked at Number One.

**ARL Chairman, Bill Buckley**

**"I've never seen a greater player than Clive Churchill"**

**\*   \*   \***

**Easts Pioneer, 'Dally' Messenger**

**"Churchill is the greatest"**

Clive was born the second son and fourth child of Herbert Hilton Churchill (1887-1967) and Vera May Ferguson (1899-1988). He was educated at St. Joseph's Convent, Mereweather, and at the Marist Brothers' High School, Hamilton. He excelled at rugby league while still at school, playing without boots, kicking with either foot (but mainly the left), and developed a quicksilver elusiveness at five-eighth, and occasionally as fullback. His nickname in those early days was 'Tigger'. In 1945 he started playing with Central Newcastle, and in 1947 he concentrated more on his fullback role, which presented him with a Country Seconds jersey against City Seconds in June. Country won by 25-22 and Churchill kicked four goals in the match, which impressed the Rabbitohs and their patron David Spring quickly signed him up for £12 10s a win and nothing for a loss.

Churchill at first lived in Mascot and played the last two games of the 1947 season for Souths. He fitted perfectly into the traditional free-flowing style of the Rabbitohs and soon shone at fullback. In 1948 he represented the City Seconds side, which defeated Country Seconds 19-3, and then got his first state jumper against Queensland in Game 4 at Brisbane Cricket Ground. The Maroons won the previous match and NSW selectors were not happy with the loss and they were looking towards the future. With Churchill playing a starring role in his state debut the Blues won 17-13 and at the end of that season he was rewarded to go on his first Kangaroo tour. During 1949, Churchill received an enormous offer from Workington Town of £10,000 per season, but he couldn't beat the international transfer ban in place at the time. He later described it as being 'robbed of the chance' to earn the large sum of money at the peak of his career.

On 19 June 1950 he had married Shirley Grace Berriman, a dressmaker, at St. Francis's Catholic Church, Paddington. He needed to supplement his income, and throughout the 1950s he was working in many different jobs, including a storeman, sports-store salesman, garage proprietor and taxi driver.

In July 1950, Churchill led Australia to their first 'Ashes' win against Great Britain since 1920. But at his club he had to play under Jack Rayner's captaincy, who was also coach, which never bothered him. In September 1950 he won his first premiership at the Rabbitohs and then played in the next four grand finals for the club, winning all except the controversial 1952 grand final. There were claims made that Wests scored two tries from forward passes and that the Rabbitohs had two fair tries disallowed. Jack Rayner later said they were not allowed to win and he never spoke to the referee again. Churchill missed the 1955 grand final after breaking his wrist in Round 17 against Manly, which remains one of the most legendary efforts in the Greatest Game of All. He heard his arms snap when he made a courageous tackle early in the first half. At half-time he was given a pain killer and his arm was wrapped in a cardboard cover from an exercise book. With his arm hanging limply by his side, Churchill kicked the last goal of the match to seal victory over Manly by 9-7. What the Rabbitohs achieved in season 1955 has been called "The Miracle of 1955". They had to win 11 sudden-death matches to claim their sixteenth premiership.

His successful coaching career commenced in 1958 when he took over from Jack Rayner as captain-coach of Souths. He also got divorced in June of that year. On 31 January 1959 he married Joyce Ivy Martin and took on a captain-coaching role with Brisbane Norths, who won their first premiership under his leadership since 1950 after defeating Brothers 24-18 in the grand final. He also captain-coached Brisbane and Queensland against the touring New Zealand Kiwis side in 1959. From there he went to captain-coach Moree in 1960-61, as well as coaching City Firsts and Seconds, Sydney, NSW and Australia, all in 1960.

He released his autobiography as told to Jim Mathers in 1962, titled: "They Called Me The Little Master: Clive Churchill's Colourful Story". In 1964-65 he coached Canterbury-Bankstown and then Easts Brisbane pulled off a major coup for the game in Queensland when they signed 'The Little Master' as a non-playing coach for 1965-66. Churchill was also chased by Tweed Seagulls after expressing his desire to live on the Gold Coast one day.

He finally returned to the Rabbitohs for his second stint in 1967, this time as their coach, where he won four premierships and lost one which has been hailed as the biggest ever upset in history – the 1969 grand final loss to Balmain, where the Tigers used controversial tactics of laying down 'injured' to slow down the Rabbitohs rhythm and give their forwards a breather.

With the Rabbitohs experiencing financial difficulties in the early 1970s they lost so many of their star players to other clubs and Churchill's reign as coach came to an end in 1975, when he resigned late in the season after a bleak year.

In 1978 he was appointed coach of the Gold Coast representative side to contest the Carlton Cup competition, which also involved Toowoomba, Ipswich and Wide Bay. The Queenslanders also made it to the second round of the mid-week Amco Cup knock-out competition in the same year under Churchill's guidance.

In January 1982 he was seriously injured when his liquor shop was robbed at Randwick, and in December 1984 he was hospitalised with cancer. In May that year about $50,000 was raised for his family at a packed testimonial dinner. The following month he was appointed Member of the Order of Australia (AM) in the Queen's Birthday Honours. He died in 1985 at Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, Camperdown, aged 58, with several thousand people attending his funeral at St. Mary's Cathedral in Sydney's Central Business District. His legacy is remembered by naming of the 'Clive Churchill Stand' at the SCG, and the awarding of the 'Clive Churchill Medal' to the best player in the grand final.

Throughout his illustrious career it was assumed by many League followers that 'The Little Master' was of Indigenous background. He was a bit dark skinned and had dark, curly hair. But his son Rod confirmed in 2023 that all those rumours were not true, and that his father was not Indigenous.

name: Ron Coote, AM
bio:
**Full Name:** Ronald Joseph Coote, AM.

**Nickname:** Solid, Cootey.

**Born:** 25/10/1944 at Kingsford (Sydney), NSW.

**Juniors:** Kensington United, Marcellin College Randwick.

**Junior Reps:** Souths' Jersey Flegg U-17s 1961, Souths' Presidents Cup U-21s 1963 (premiers).

**Height:** 188 cm.

**Weight:** 85-92 kgs.

**PLAYING CAREER WITH THE RABBITOHS**

**Rabbitohs First Grade Player No:** 528. **Rabbitohs First Grade Captain No:** 65.

First Grade premiership 1965-71 (149 games, 48 tries, 147 points) - 1 game as captain.

First Grade Pre-Season Cup 1964-70 (27 games, 10 tries, 30 pts)

First Grade State Cup 1964 (1 game, 0 pts)

First Grade Australasian Club Championship Tour 1968 (1 tour game, 0 pts)

First Grade Domestic Tour 1964 (2 tour games, 2 tries, 6 points)

Reserve Grade premiership 1963 (5 games, 1 try, 3 points)

Third Grade premiership 1963 (1 game, 0 pts)

**FIRST GRADE PREMIERSHIP DEBUT**

Round 1 v Easts at the Sydney Sports Ground, Moore Park/Gadigal-Bidjigal, April 5, 1964 – Age: 19 years and 163 days.

**REPRESENTATIVE HONOURS WITH THE RABBITOHS**

**Kangaroos Player No:** 417. **NSW Blues Player No:** 588.

Australia 1967-70 (17 Tests, 7 tries, 21 points/19 other games, 9 tries, 27 points)

NSW 1965-70 (8 games v Queensland, 6 tries, 18 points/1 other game, 0 pts)

City Firsts 1969 (1 game, 1 try, 3 points)

City Seconds 1965, 1967-68 (3 games, 3 tries, 9 points)

NSW Colts 1964 (1 game, 1 try, 3 points)

Sydney Colts 1964 (1 game, 0 pts)

**AWARDS & ACCOLADES**

1967 Telegraph-TCN9 Player of the Year

1967 Rabbitohs Club Player of the Year

1967 Rabbitohs premiership winner

1967-68 Kangaroo Tour

1968 Rabbitohs premiership winner

1968-69 E.E. Christensen Rugby League Yearbook Player of the Year

1970 Rabbitohs premiership winner

1970, 1974 Harry Sunderland Medal (for best player in Ashes Tests)

1971 Rabbitohs premiership winner

1971 Grand Final Man of the match (Clive Churchill Medal)

1974 Easts Sydney premiership winner

1975 Easts Sydney Mid-Week Knock-Out (Amco) Cup winner

1975 Easts Sydney premiership winner

1990 Rabbitohs Life Membership (Inductee No. 65)

1995 Member of the Order of Australia (AM)

2003 Sydney Cricket Ground Life Membership

2004 Rabbitohs Dream Team (as Lock)

2005 NSWRL Life Membership

2005 NRL Hall of Fame (Inductee No. 71)

2005 International Rugby League Hall of Fame

2008 South Sydney Juniors Team of the Century (as Lock and Captain)

2012 Ronald McDonald House Hall of Fame

2013 Sport Australia Hall of Fame

2022 Ron Coote Stand at Allianz Stadium (named after him)

2024 Immortal (Inductee No. 14)

**ADMINISTRATIVE CAREER**

Judiciary Committee 1992-2002

NSWRL Metropolitan Selection Committee (Deputy) 1988-91

Co-Founded Men of League Foundation (with Jim Hall) 2002

Chairman of Men of League Foundation 2002-16 (name changed to Family of League)

Honorary President of Men/Family of League Foundation 2017-present

**BIOGRAPHY**

Ron Coote, the Prince of Locks, was finally recognised as a rugby league Immortal. Forty-six years after he hung up his boots, the former Rabbitohs and Roosters champion lock forward has been elevated to the highest echelon of rugby league greatness, after being named the 14th Immortal in an emotional ceremony in Sydney on 21st August 2024. The 79-year-old Coote was emotional as he received a standing ovation at the SCG when his name was read out by Prime Minister Anthony Albanese. Every person in the room stood up to applaud Coote as he made his way to the stage where he swapped his Hall of Fame jacket for an Immortal jacket.

"I'm a bit taken for words at the moment," Coote said before pausing to hold back tears.

"I never thought I'd be crying at 80, but I am."

"To me it's just sensational, I never thought it'd happen. I've been at this function a few times and this time I got the gong so that's really good to me, I really appreciate it. Thank you", said Coote.

It was 43 years ago when he was first considered in the inaugural intake to become an Immortal, which is the same length of time it took the Rabbitohs to break their premiership drought.

**Nicholas Pappas AM**

**Rabbitohs Chairman**

**"It is difficult to put into words Ron Coote's impact on the South Sydney Rabbitohs**

**and the game of Rugby League.**

**A giant in every sense, Ron embodies all the qualities of a sporting legend: towering strength, uncanny skill and gracious humility"**

Ron's parents John 'Jack' Joseph Coote (1907-1986) and Doreen Clarice Channells (1909-1996) had four kids, three boys and a girl. Ron was the third oldest son. His father Jack played for Easts between 1926-37, but he was a South Sydney junior and even played in one game for the Rabbitohs Presidents Cup side in 1925, where he scored a try in their first round loss to Easts, by 9-5. They lived in Meeks Street, Kingsford, which was divided between Souths and Easts boundaries, and when Easts found out Jack lived on their side of the boundary, they were quick to sign him up after the Presidents Cup game.

Jack's two other brothers (Ron's uncles) also played the greatest game of all: William Francis Coote (1905-1995) played lower grades for Easts in the late 1920s, and Douglas Evan Coote (1910-1997) played lower grades for Souths in 1936.

Ron's older brother Peter Robert Coote (1942-2017) is the father of Daniel Coote, who played for the Rabbitohs between 1991-94 in one first grade game. Daniel currently lives in Perth.

Ron went to three different primary schools, St. Michael's Daceyville, Marist Brothers Randwick and Rainbow Street Public School Randwick, he then did three years of high schooling with the South Sydney Junior Technical High School at Maroubra, which was renamed South Sydney Boys' High School in 1959. He played his junior footy with Kensington United and at Marcellin College Randwick, playing against the likes of George Piggins, Bob McCarthy and many other great Rabbitoh players who came from that wonderful era and were part of the great South Sydney Junior League nursery.

Coote partnered Bob McCarthy in the forwards of Souths' Jersey Flegg Under-17s side in 1961, and in 1963 he played in the Presidents Cup Under-21s final for Souths against St. George, which the Rabbitohs won by 7-5. Souths graded him after the Presidents Cup victory and he made his first grade debut the following year in round 1 against Easts, scoring a nice try on debut in the Rabbitohs 17-8 victory.

Coote's tall, long-legged frame showcased his great running style complemented by devastating cover defence. Although Coote's early representative career was restricted because of Johnny Raper he did make his NSW debut in 1965, the year Souths made the grand final. He was a key member of the champion Rabbitohs teams that won grand finals in 1967-68 & 1970-71 – a run interrupted only by the shock loss to Balmain in 1969 - and his stature as a Test star grew during this period. He toured with the 1967-68 Kangaroos, playing in two Tests against Great Britain (the first as lock and then as a second rower) and all three Tests against France.

After playing in the 1968 World Cup and touring NZ, Coote's career highlight came after the three-Test series against Great Britain in 1970. Injuries to Graeme Langlands and John Sattler saw Coote named captain of Australia's World Cup team that ultimately won a brutal final against Great Britain at Leeds. It was later dubbed "The Battle of Headingley" and everybody was shocked as to why it took 79 minutes to get somebody sent off the field, after 14 cautions were given previously.

Ronnie also took on a beer truck in 1970, which he enjoyed but regretted later because he had to decline playing representative football between 1971 and 1973 owing to his commitment towards his job. Also, during that year he had a constant visitor to his home in Maroubra when he returned either from training or work. Living just a couple of streets away was then-Roosters coach, Don Furner, who happened to pop in for a regular cuppa with Robyn, Ron's wife. Donny was building a team at the Roosters and he wanted Ron there.

Coote signed to play with Easts from 1972 onwards, and many Rabbitoh fans regarded his defection across Anzac Parade to their arch-rivals as high treason. In fact, it would be fair to say he was seen as public enemy number one with some of the Rabbitohs fans at the time. His defection kickstarted the infamous "Book of Feuds" - to which Souths co-owner Russell Crowe has added many chapters since. A woman claiming to be a gypsy called Coote – and told him she would put a gypsy curse on him that would see bad luck follow him for the rest of his life. Another lady called and told him that her family had a pet rabbit called Ron Coote – and the day he signed with Easts, they cooked him and had him for lunch. But Coote remained defiant, claiming the switch was more about principle than money. "A lot of clubs didn't look after players back then - I know we at Souths were getting a lot less than guys at clubs like Easts and Manly. Easts offered me good money and they were building a strong team, so it made sense to go - you have to look after your family financially", he said later.

Not many people remember this, but Coote rejected a $70,000 three-year offer from the QRL in January 1972, which was the biggest deal in the code's history at the time. Major sponsors behind the offer were Carlton United Brewery, and it also carried with it the promise of hotel management when he retired. Coote gave family reasons for his decision not to go there, with his children's schooling paramount, and the idea of owning a pub did not appeal to him at the time.

Coote added to his four titles he won at Souths with two more premierships at the Roosters, winning back-to-back grand finals in 1974-75 – between 1965 to 1975 he played in every grand final except two (1966 and 1973), nine in eleven years and winning six of them. A remarkable effort indeed. He also became the first player in history to play 100 games for two separate clubs. During that time he also sold his beer truck to begin work with Evercraft, in a sales role which also allowed him to return to representative football. But two broken arms in 1976, a staph infection, and another bad arm injury two years later led to his eventual retirement from playing the game. He said later in a Rugby League Week interview the only reason why he played in those last two years of his career was to help out Artie Beetson, who was his coach at Easts.

In his weekly column of the 1978 Rugby League Week Ron was one of the first critics to advocate for tries to be increased in value, from three points to four. The League did eventually change the value of a try in 1983. Coote was the player other players wanted to be on the field with and who coaches wanted to coach. The attacking partnership he formed with Bob McCarthy at the Rabbitohs during their golden run will long be remembered by their army of fans.

In 1981 he began his very successful association with the McDonalds franchise when he owned several well-known stores throughout Sydney and currently his family owns three of them. His contributions to the game continued in retirement, serving as a member of the NSWRL's Judiciary throughout the 1990s, before what many people believe was his biggest achievement when he established the Men/Family of League Foundation in 2002 with Jim Hall, which has gone from strength to strength in supporting former players, officials and referees who have fallen on hard times.

Ron and his wife retired nicely at Lake Conjola on the NSW south coast, and after 38 years of living there peacefully were unfortunate when their house was destroyed in those terrible 2019 New Year's Eve bushfires. He thought he had the fire under control and while he was hosing water around the house some stray embers had gone under the roof of the house and suddenly there was smoke coming out from under the roof. His wife Robyn and daughter Natalie had to run out and jump into the lake to flee the fires. They spent about an hour in the lake before a passer-by came to save them on a jet ski. His daughter's house next door was also destroyed, along with 50 other homes in Lake Conjola. They lost everything, including all of Ron's lifelong memorabilia items which he cherished. But since that terrible event they have rebuilt their house and still enjoy living there.

Coote still loves the game that has given him so much throughout his life, and he's a big fan of Peter V'landys AM and what he's done for the game.
